title: Executive Order 12724—Blocking Iraqi Government Property and Prohibiting Transactions With Iraq

flags.justification: Broad blocking authority and wide implementation discretion for the Secretary of the Treasury, now expanded relative to EO 12722 with a broader definition of covered Iraqi entities.
No review process for affected persons is specified in this order's own text.
Multiple prohibited-transaction categories all coherently serve one purpose: implementing UN Security Council Resolution 661's comprehensive embargo.
Sec. 2(h)'s catch-all anti-evasion provision is broad and open-ended, consistent with EO 12722.
No incentive structure rewarding behavior contrary to the order's purpose is identifiable.
No review or reauthorization mechanism is specified despite being tied to an ongoing declared emergency.
The order is explicitly transmitted to Congress.
No external check is specified on the Secretary of the Treasury's exercise of blocking authority.

This formally implements a binding United Nations Security Council resolution (No. 661) into U.S. domestic law — a significant, textually explicit connection between domestic sanctions authority and multilateral international action.
Sec. 6 carefully and explicitly manages the transition from EO 12722, revoking it only 'to the extent inconsistent' and preserving prior administrative actions — a clean, deliberate supersession.
The humanitarian and UN-official-business exceptions (Sec. 2(b), 2(d)(ii)) are narrow and purpose-consistent.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 6/22 = 27.3%. Issued one week after EO 12722, expanding and formalizing the sanctions in direct implementation of UN Security Council Resolution 661.
